***Papaver somniferum***, commonly known as the **opium poppy**[[2]](#cite_note-PlantAtlas-2) or **breadseed poppy**,[[3]](#cite_note-UniWisconsin-3) is a species of [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") in the family [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"). It is the species of plant from which both [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") and [poppy seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed") are derived and is also a valuable [ornamental plant](/wiki/Ornamental_plant "Ornamental plant") grown in gardens. Its native range is the western [Mediterranean region](/wiki/Mediterranean_region "Mediterranean region"), but has since been obscured by widespread [introduction](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species") and [cultivation](/wiki/Horticulture "Horticulture") since ancient times to the present day. It is now [naturalised](/wiki/Naturalisation_(biology) "Naturalisation (biology)") across much of the world with temperate climates.[[4]](#cite_note-POWO-4)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

*Papaver somniferum* is an [annual](/wiki/Annual_plant "Annual plant") herb growing to about 100 centimetres (40 inches) tall. The plant is strongly [glaucous](/wiki/Glaucous "Glaucous"), giving a greyish-green appearance, and the stem and [leaves](/wiki/Leaves "Leaves") bear a sparse distribution of coarse hairs. The large leaves are lobed, the upper stem leaves clasping the stem,[[5]](#cite_note-Stace-2019-5) the lowest leaves with a short petiole.[[6]](#cite_note-Clapham-1981-6): 40 The flowers are up to 8–18 cm (3–7 in) diameter, normally with four red, white, or mauve petals, often with a dark patch at the base of each petal.[[7]](#cite_note-Streeter-7) The fruit is a hairless, rounded capsule topped with 12–18 radiating [stigmatic](/wiki/Stigma_(botany) "Stigma (botany)") rays, or fluted cap.[[8]](#cite_note-Reader's_Digest-1981-8) All parts of the plant exude white latex when wounded.[[5]](#cite_note-Stace-2019-5): 93[[9]](#cite_note-Blamey-2003-9): 32

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

The native range of opium poppy is probably [Macaronesia](/wiki/Macaronesia "Macaronesia"), W. & Central Mediterranean [[10]](#cite_note-10), but extensive cultivation and introduction of the species throughout Europe since ancient times have obscured its origin. It has [escaped from cultivation](/wiki/Escaped_plant "Escaped plant"), or has been introduced and become naturalised extensively in all regions of the British Isles, particularly in the south and east, with its seeds having been found in archaeological material dating back to the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[2]](#cite_note-PlantAtlas-2) and in almost all other countries of the world with suitable, temperate climates.[[11]](#cite_note-GBIF.org-11)

## Taxonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Taxonomy")]

*Papaver somniferum* was given its [accepted name](/wiki/Accepted_name "Accepted name") by the Swedish botanist [Carl Linnaeus](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus") on page 508 of his *[Species Plantarum](/wiki/Species_Plantarum "Species Plantarum")* in 1753.[[4]](#cite_note-POWO-4)[[12]](#cite_note-12) It is classified in the genus *[Papaver](/wiki/Papaver "Papaver")* within the larger family [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"). It has two accepted [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies "Subspecies") and one natural [hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_(biology) "Hybrid (biology)") between them:[[4]](#cite_note-POWO-4)

* *Papaver somniferum* nothosubsp. *authemanii*  
  This natural hybrid is native to France. It was first described as a hybrid named *Papaver* × *authemanii* in 1893 and reclassified to its accepted name in 2012.[[13]](#cite_note-POWO_nothosubsp-13)
* *Papaver somniferum* subsp. *setigerum*  
  This subspecies was first named *Papaver setigerum* in 1815 by [Augustin Pyramus de Candolle](/wiki/Augustin_Pyramus_de_Candolle "Augustin Pyramus de Candolle"). It was reclassified as a subspecies in 1882 by [Giovanni Arcangeli](/wiki/Giovanni_Arcangeli "Giovanni Arcangeli"). Its native range is North Africa from Morocco to Lybia and in southern Europe from Portugual to Italy.[[14]](#cite_note-POWO_subsp_setigerum-14)
* *Papaver somniferum* subsp. *somniferum*   
  The [autonymic](/wiki/Autonym_(botany) "Autonym (botany)") subspecies is a [cultigen](/wiki/Cultigen "Cultigen"), a plant that has been developed by human selection.[[15]](#cite_note-POWO_subsp_somniferum-15)

The opium poppy has 76 [synonyms](/wiki/Synonym_(botany) "Synonym (botany)") of its two subspecies and the hybrid.[[13]](#cite_note-POWO_nothosubsp-13)[[14]](#cite_note-POWO_subsp_setigerum-14)[[15]](#cite_note-POWO_subsp_somniferum-15)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Uses")]

This poppy is grown as an agricultural crop on a large scale, for one of three primary purposes; to produce [poppy seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed"), to produce [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") for use mainly by the pharmaceutical industry,[[16]](#cite_note-Bradsher-2014-16) and to produce other [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") (mainly [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") and [oripavine](/wiki/Oripavine "Oripavine")) that are processed by pharmaceutical companies into drugs such as [hydrocodone](/wiki/Hydrocodone "Hydrocodone") and [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone").[[16]](#cite_note-Bradsher-2014-16) Each of these goals has special breeds that are targeted at one of these businesses, and breeding efforts (including [biotechnological](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") ones) are continually underway.[[16]](#cite_note-Bradsher-2014-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Gaevskii-1999-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Chaturvedi-2014-18) A comparatively small amount of *P. somniferum* seed is also produced commercially for ornamental purposes.

Today many [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar") have been bred that do not produce a significant quantity of opium.[[3]](#cite_note-UniWisconsin-3)[[17]](#cite_note-Gaevskii-1999-17) The cultivar 'Sujata' produces no [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") at all.[[18]](#cite_note-Chaturvedi-2014-18) This differentiation has strong implications for legal policy surrounding the growing of the plant.[[17]](#cite_note-Gaevskii-1999-17)

## Biology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Biology")]

### Metabolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Metabolism")]

The alkaloids are organic nitrogenous compounds, derivatives of [secondary metabolism](/wiki/Secondary_metabolism "Secondary metabolism"), synthesised through the metabolic pathway of benzylisoquinoline.[[19]](#cite_note-Ye-2018-19) First, the [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") [phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine"), through the enzyme [phenylalanine hydroxylase](/wiki/Phenylalanine_hydroxylase "Phenylalanine hydroxylase"), is transformed into [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"). Tyrosine can follow two different routes: by tyrosine hydroxylase it can form L-dopamine ([L-DOPA](/wiki/L-DOPA "L-DOPA")), or it can be reduced to form 4-phenylhydroxyacetaldehyde (4-HPAA). Subsequently, L-DOPA reacts with 4-HPAA and, through a series of reactions, forms (S) -norcoclaurine, which carries the benzylisoquinoline skeleton that gives its name to this pathway. The conversion of (S) -norcoclaurin to (S) -reticuline is one of the key points, since from (S) -reticuline morphine can be formed through the morphinan route, noscapine through the path of the noscapina or berberina.[[19]](#cite_note-Ye-2018-19)

### Genome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Genome")]

The poppy genome contains 51,213 genes encoding proteins distributed 81.6% in 11 individual chromosomes and 18.4% remaining in unplaced scaffolds.[[19]](#cite_note-Ye-2018-19) In addition, 70.9% of the genome is made up of repetitive elements, of which the most represented are the long terminal repeat [retrotransposons](/wiki/Retrotransposons "Retrotransposons"). This enrichment of genes is related to the maintenance of homeostasis and a positive regulation of [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(biology) "Transcription (biology)").[[19]](#cite_note-Ye-2018-19)

The analysis of synergy of the opium poppy reveals traces of segmental duplications 110 million years ago (MYA), before the divergence between *Papaveraceae* and Ranunculaceae, and an event of duplication of the complete genome makes 7.8 MYA.

The genes are possibly grouped as follows:[[19]](#cite_note-Ye-2018-19)

* The genes responsible for the conversion of (S) -reticuline to noscapine are found on chromosome 11.
* The genes responsible for the conversion of (S) -reticuline to thebaine are found on chromosome 11.
* The genes responsible for the conversion of thebaine are found in chromosome 1, chromosome 2, chromosome 7, and perhaps others.

### Cultivars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cultivars")]

*P. somniferum* has had a very long tradition of use, starting in the Neolithic. This long period of time allowed the development of a broad range of different forms. In total there are 52 botanical varieties.[[20]](#cite_note-Dittbrenner-2009-20) Breeding of *P. somniferum* faces a challenge caused by the contradictory breeding goals for this species.[[20]](#cite_note-Dittbrenner-2009-20) On one hand a very high content of alkaloids is requested for medical uses. Global demand for the alkaloids and their pharmaceutical derivatives has increased in recent years. Therefore, there is a need for the development of [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar") with a high opium yield.[[21]](#cite_note-Yadav-2006-21) On the other hand, the food industry demands alkaloid contents be as low as possible.[[20]](#cite_note-Dittbrenner-2009-20)

There are also many ornamental cultivars selected for colour or number of their petals or other characteristics. There are [double-flowered](/wiki/Double-flowered "Double-flowered") cultivars, often called peony flowered or 'paeoniflorum' in a range of colours. There are even varieties selected for the shape of their pods such as 'Hen and Chickens' with smaller pods around a larger central pod.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ecology")]

### Diseases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Diseases")]

*P. somniferum* is susceptible to several fungal, insect and virus infections including seed borne diseases such as [downy mildew](/wiki/Downy_mildew "Downy mildew") and [root rot](/wiki/Root_rot "Root rot"). The use of [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide") in combination to [cultural methods](/wiki/Cultural_methods "Cultural methods") have been considered as major control measures for various poppy diseases.[[23]](#cite_note-Singh-2016-23)

The fungal pathogen *[Peronospora arborescens](/wiki/Peronospora_arborescens "Peronospora arborescens")*, the causal agent of downy mildew, occurs preferentially during wet and humid conditions.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24) This [oomycete](/wiki/Oomycete "Oomycete") penetrates the roots through [oospores](/wiki/Oospore "Oospore") and infects the leaves as [conidia](/wiki/Conidia "Conidia") in a secondary infection.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The fungus causes [hypertrophy](/wiki/Hypertrophy "Hypertrophy") and curvature of the stem and flower stalks.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The symptoms are [chlorosis](/wiki/Chlorosis "Chlorosis") and curling of the affected tissues with necrotic spots.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The leaf under-surface is covered with a downy mildew coating containing [conidiospores](/wiki/Conidium "Conidium") that spread the infection further leading to plant damage and death.[[28]](#cite_note-Kapoor-1995-28) Another downy mildew species, *[Peronospora somniferi](/wiki/Peronospora "Peronospora")*, produces systemic infections leading to stunting and deformation of poppy plants.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Downy mildew can be controlled preventively at the initial stage of seed development through several fungicide applications.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24)

Leaf blight caused by the fungus *[Helminthosporium papaveris](/wiki/Helminthosporium_solani "Helminthosporium solani")* is one of the most destructive poppy diseases worldwide. The seed-borne fungus causes root rot in young plants and stunted stems in plants at a higher development stage, where leaf spots appear on the leaves and is being transmitted to capsules and seeds.[[28]](#cite_note-Kapoor-1995-28) Early sowing of seeds and deep plowing of poppy residues can reduce fungal inoculum during the plant growing season in the following year on neighboring poppy stocks, respectively.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24)

[Mosaic diseases](/wiki/Mosaic_disease "Mosaic disease") in *P. somniferum* are caused by [rattle virus](/wiki/Tobacco_rattle_virus "Tobacco rattle virus") and the [Carlavirus](/wiki/Carlavirus "Carlavirus").[[23]](#cite_note-Singh-2016-23) In 2006, a novel virus tentatively called "opium poppy mosaic virus" (OPMV) from the genus *[Umbravirus](/wiki/Umbravirus "Umbravirus")* was isolated from *P. somniferum* containing leaf mosaic and mottling symptoms, in New Zealand.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Pests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pests")]

There are only a few pests that can do harm to *P. somniferum*.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24)[*[example needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:AUDIENCE "Wikipedia:AUDIENCE")*]

[Flea beetles](/wiki/Flea_beetle "Flea beetle") perforate the leaves of young plants and aphids suck on the sap of the flower buds.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24) The poppy root weevil (*[Stenocarus ruficornis](/w/index.php?title=Stenocarus_ruficornis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stenocarus ruficornis (page does not exist)")*) is another significant pest. The insect lives in the soil and migrates in spring to the poppy fields after crop emergence. Adults damage the leaves of small plants by eating them. Female lay their eggs into the tissue of lower leaves. Insect larvae hatch and burrow into the soil to complete their life cycle on the poppy roots as adults.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Cultivation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cultivation")]

In the growth development of *P. somniferum*, six stages can be distinguished. The growth development starts with the growth of the seedlings. In a second step the rosette-type leaves and stalks are formed. After that budding (hook stage) takes place as a third step. The hook stage is followed by flowering. Subsequently, technical maturity is reached, which means that the plant is ready for cutting. The last step is biological maturity; dry seeds are ripened. The photoperiod seems to be the main determinant of flower development of *P. somniferum*.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

*P. somniferum* shows a very slow development in the beginning of its vegetation period. Due to this fact the competition of weeds is very high in early stages. It is very important to control weeds effectively in the first 50 days after sowing.[[33]](#cite_note-Gaur-1986-33) Additionally, *Papaver somniferum* is rather susceptible to herbicides. The pre-emergence application of the herbicide chlortoluron has been shown to be effective in reducing weed levels.[[33]](#cite_note-Gaur-1986-33) However, in the last decade the weed management of *Papaver somniferum* has shifted from pre-emergence treatments to post-emergence treatments.[[34]](#cite_note-Pinke-2014-34) Especially, the application of the two herbicides mesotrione and tembotrione has become very popular. The combined application of those two herbicides has been shown to be recommendable for effective weed management in *Papaver somniferum*.[[34]](#cite_note-Pinke-2014-34) Sowing time (autumn or spring), preceding crop and soil texture are important variables influencing the weed [species composition](/wiki/Species_composition "Species composition"). A highly abundant weed species in *Papaver somniferum* fields was shown to be *[Papaver rhoeas](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas")*.[[34]](#cite_note-Pinke-2014-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Pinke-2011-35) *Papaver somniferum* and *Papaver rhoeas* are [congeners](/wiki/Biological_specificity "Biological specificity") and belong to the same plant family, which impedes the chemical control of this weed species.[[35]](#cite_note-Pinke-2011-35) Therefore, weed management represents a big challenge and requires technological knowledge from the farmer.[[35]](#cite_note-Pinke-2011-35) In order to increase the efficiency of weed control not only chemical weed control should be applied but also mechanical weed control.[[35]](#cite_note-Pinke-2011-35)

For *P. somniferum*, a growth density of 70 to 80 plants per square metre is recommended.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Latex-to-biomass yield is greatest under conditions of slight water deficit.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### As an ornamental plant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: As an ornamental plant")]

Live plants and seeds of the opium poppy are widely sold by seed companies and nurseries in most of the western world, including the United States. Poppies are sought after by gardeners for the vivid colour of the flowers, the hardiness and reliability of the poppy plants, the exotic chocolate-vegetal fragrance note of some cultivars,[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] and the ease of growing the plants from purchased flats of seedlings or by direct sowing of the seed. Poppy seed pods are also sold for dried flower arrangements.

Though "opium poppy and poppy straw" are listed in Schedule II of the United States' [Controlled Substances Act](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act "Controlled Substances Act"), *P. somniferum* can be grown legally in the United States as a seed crop or as an [ornamental plant](/wiki/Ornamental_plant "Ornamental plant").[[38]](#cite_note-38) During the summer, opium poppies can be seen flowering in gardens throughout North America and Europe, and displays are found in many private plantings, as well as in public botanical and museum gardens such as [United States Botanical Garden](/wiki/United_States_Botanical_Garden "United States Botanical Garden"), [Missouri Botanical Garden](/wiki/Missouri_Botanical_Garden "Missouri Botanical Garden"), and [North Carolina Botanical Garden](/wiki/North_Carolina_Botanical_Garden "North Carolina Botanical Garden").

Many countries grow the plants, and some rely heavily on the commercial production of the drug as a major source of income. As an additional source of profit, the seeds of the same plants are sold for use in foods, so the cultivation of the plant is a significant source of income. This international trade in seeds of *P. somniferum* was addressed by a UN resolution "to fight the international trade in illicit opium poppy seeds" on 28 July 1998.

#### Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Food")]

In 2018, world production of poppy seeds for consumption was 76,240 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne"), led by [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") with 35% of the world total (table). Poppy seed production and [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade") are susceptible to fluctuations mainly due to unstable yields. The performance of most [genotypes](/wiki/Genotype "Genotype") of *Papaver somniferum* is very susceptible to environmental changes.[[40]](#cite_note-40) This behaviour led to a stagnation of the poppy seed market value between 2008 and 2009 as a consequence of high [stock](/wiki/Stock "Stock") levels, bad weather and poor quality.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The world leading importer of poppy seed is [India](/wiki/India "India") (16 000 tonnes), followed by [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Poppy seed oil remains a niche product due to the lower yield compared to conventional oil crops.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

#### Medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Medicine")]

Australia (Tasmania), Turkey and India are the major producers of poppy for medicinal purposes and poppy-based drugs, such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") or [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[21]](#cite_note-Yadav-2006-21) *The New York Times* reported, in 2014, that [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") was the largest producer of the poppy cultivars used for [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") (85% of the world's supply) and [oripavine](/wiki/Oripavine "Oripavine") (100% of the world's supply) production. Tasmania also had 25% of the world's opium and codeine production.[[16]](#cite_note-Bradsher-2014-16)

### Restrictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Restrictions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Poppy_Fields_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1361923.jpg/330px-Poppy_Fields_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1361923.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poppy_Fields_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1361923.jpg)

Opium poppy fields near [Metheringham](/wiki/Metheringham "Metheringham"), Lincolnshire, England. Also visible in the field are red [field poppies](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas"), another related species of poppy which often grows alongside *P. somniferum* as a weed

In most of [Central and Eastern Europe](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_Europe "Central and Eastern Europe"), poppy seed is commonly used for traditional pastries and cakes, and it is legal to grow poppies throughout the region, although Germany requires a license.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Since January 1999 in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), according to the *167/1998 Sb. Addictive Substances Act*, poppies growing in fields larger than 100 square metres (120 sq yd) is obliged for reporting to the local Custom Office.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Extraction of opium from the plants is prohibited by law (§ 15 letter d/ of the act). It is also prohibited to grow varieties with more than 0.8% of morphine in dry matter of their [capsules](/wiki/Capsule_(fruit) "Capsule (fruit)"), excluding research and experimental purposes (§24/1b/ of the act). The name *Czech blue poppy* refers to blue poppy seeds used for food.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The United Kingdom does not require a license for opium poppy cultivation, but does for extracting opium for medicinal products.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

When the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") attempted to ban the cultivation of *Papaver somniferum* by private individuals on a small scale (such as personal gardens), citizens in EU countries where poppy seed is eaten heavily, such as countries in the Central-Eastern region, strongly resisted the plan, causing the EU to change course.

In the United States, opium poppies and poppy straw are prohibited.[[49]](#cite_note-49) As the opium poppy is legal for [culinary](/wiki/Culinary "Culinary") or [aesthetic](/wiki/Aesthetic "Aesthetic") reasons, poppies were once grown as a cash crop by farmers in California. The law of poppy cultivation in the United States is somewhat ambiguous.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The reason for the ambiguity is that the Opium Poppy Control Act of 1942 (now [repealed](/wiki/Repeal "Repeal"))[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) stated that any opium poppies should be declared illegal, except if the farmers were issued a state [permit](/wiki/License "License"). § 3 of the Opium Poppy Control Act stated:

> It shall be unlawful for any person who is not the holder of a license authorizing him to produce the opium poppy, duly issued to him by the Secretary of the Treasury in accordance with the provisions of this Act, to produce the opium poppy, or to permit the production of the opium poppy in or upon any place owned, occupied, used, or controlled by him.

This led to the Poppy Rebellion, and to the Narcotics Bureau arresting anyone planting opium poppies and forcing the destruction of poppy fields of anyone who defied the prohibition of poppy cultivation.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Though the press of those days favoured the [Federal Bureau of Narcotics](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Narcotics "Federal Bureau of Narcotics"), the state of California supported the farmers who grew opium poppies for their seeds for uses in foods such as poppy seed [muffins](/wiki/Muffin "Muffin"). Today, this area of law has remained vague and remains somewhat controversial in the United States.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The Opium Poppy Control Act of 1942 was repealed on 27 October 1970.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Under the Federal [Controlled Substances Act](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act "Controlled Substances Act"), opium poppy and poppy straw are listed as [Schedule II drugs](/wiki/List_of_Schedule_II_drugs_(US) "List of Schedule II drugs (US)") under ACSN 9630. Most (all?)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] states also use this classification under the uniform penal code. Possession of a Schedule II drug is a federal and state felony.

Canada forbids possessing, seeking or obtaining the opium poppy (*Papaver somniferum*), its preparations, derivatives, alkaloids and salts, although an exception is made for poppy seed.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In some parts of Australia, *P. somniferum* is illegal to cultivate, but in [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), about 50% of the world supply is cultivated.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), it is legal to cultivate the opium poppy as long as it is not used to produce controlled drugs.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [UAE](/wiki/UAE "UAE"), and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") are among nations that ban even having poppy seeds, not just growing the plants for them.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") the cultivation of the opium poppy is illegal, as is possession of poppy seed. At least one man has been imprisoned for possessing poppy seed obtained from a bread roll.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The UAE has a long prison sentence for anyone possessing poppy seeds.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") bans cultivation in certain provinces. In northern Myanmar bans have ended a century-old tradition of growing the opium poppy. Between 20,000 and 30,000 former poppy farmers left the [Kokang](/wiki/Kokang "Kokang") region as a result of the ban in 2002.[[64]](#cite_note-64) People from the [Wa](/wiki/Wa_State "Wa State") region, where the ban was implemented in 2005, fled to areas where growing opium is still possible.

In [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), the cultivation of the opium poppy is strictly prohibited.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium")

Use of the opium poppy predates written history. The making and use of opium was known to the [ancient Minoans](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization").[[66]](#cite_note-66) Its sap was later named *opion* by the [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greeks "Ancient Greeks"). The English name is based on the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") adaptation of the Greek form.
Evidence of the early domestication of opium poppy has been discovered through small botanical remains found in regions of the Mediterranean and west of the [Rhine River](/wiki/Rhine_River "Rhine River"), predating circa 5000 BC.[[67]](#cite_note-67) These samples found in various [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") sites show the incredibly early cultivation and natural spread of the plant throughout western Europe.

Opium was used for treating [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma"), stomach illnesses, and bad eyesight.

Opium became a major colonial commodity, moving legally and illegally through trade networks on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), the [Colonial United States](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States"), [Qing China](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") and others.[[68]](#cite_note-Farooqui-2016-68) Members of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") saw the opium trade as an investment opportunity beginning in 1683.[[69]](#cite_note-Asthana-1954-69) In 1773, the Governor of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") established a monopoly on the production of Bengal opium, on behalf of the [East India Company administration](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India"). The cultivation and manufacture of Indian opium was further centralised and controlled through a series of acts issued between 1797 and 1949.[[69]](#cite_note-Asthana-1954-69)[[70]](#cite_note-Deming-2011-70) East India Company merchants balanced an economic deficit from the importation of [Chinese tea](/wiki/Chinese_tea "Chinese tea") by selling Indian opium which was smuggled into China in defiance of [Qing government](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") bans. This trade led to the [First](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") and [Second Opium Wars](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War").[[71]](#cite_note-Rinde-2018-71)[[70]](#cite_note-Deming-2011-70)[[68]](#cite_note-Farooqui-2016-68)[[72]](#cite_note-Mills-2016-72)

Many modern writers, particularly in the 19th century, have written on the opium poppy and its effects, notably [Thomas de Quincey](/wiki/Thomas_de_Quincey "Thomas de Quincey") in *[Confessions of an English Opium Eater](/wiki/Confessions_of_an_English_Opium_Eater "Confessions of an English Opium Eater")*.

The French Romantic composer [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") used opium for inspiration, subsequently producing his [Symphonie Fantastique](/wiki/Symphonie_Fantastique "Symphonie Fantastique"). In this work, a young artist overdoses on opium and experiences a series of visions of his unrequited love.

In the US, the [Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration") raided [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello "Monticello") estate in 1987. It removed the poppy plants that had been planted continually there since Jefferson was alive and using opium from them. Employees of the foundation also destroyed gift shop items like shirts depicting the poppy and packets of the heirloom seed.[[73]](#cite_note-AlterNet-2010-73)

### Poppy seeds and oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Poppy seeds and oil")]

Main article: [Poppy seed](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/3mohn_z01.JPG/250px-3mohn_z01.JPG)](/wiki/File:3mohn_z01.JPG)

Dried blue, grey and white poppy seeds used for pastries in Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Makowiec.jpg/250px-Makowiec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Makowiec.jpg)

Polish *[makowiec](/wiki/Makowiec_(pastry) "Makowiec (pastry)")*, a [nut roll](/wiki/Nut_roll "Nut roll") filled with poppy seed paste

Poppy seeds from *Papaver somniferum* are an important food item and the source of [poppy seed oil](/wiki/Poppyseed_oil "Poppyseed oil"), an edible oil that has many uses. The seeds contain very low levels of opiates and the oil extracted from them contains even less.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Both the oil and the seed residue also have commercial uses.

The poppy [press cake](/wiki/Press_cake "Press cake") as a residue of the oil pressing can be used as fodder for different animals such as cattle.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Especially in the time of the [molt](/wiki/Moulting "Moulting") of poultry and fancy fowls, the cake is nutritive and fits to their special needs. Next to the animal fodder, poppy offers other by-products. For example, the stem of the plant can be used for energy briquettes and [pellets](/wiki/Pellet_fuel "Pellet fuel") to heat.[[24]](#cite_note-Wilfried-2000-24)

Poppy seeds are used as a food in many cultures. They may be used whole by bakers to decorate their products or milled and mixed with sugar as a sweet filling. They have a creamy and nut-like flavour, and when used with ground coconut, the seeds provide a unique and flavour-rich curry base. They can be dry roasted and ground to be used in wet curry (curry paste) or dry curry.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Opiates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaver_somniferum&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Opiates")]

Main article: [Opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Poppies-seeds-dry.png/250px-Poppies-seeds-dry.png)](/wiki/File:Poppies-seeds-dry.png)

Dried poppy seed pods and stems (plate), and seeds (bowl)

The opium poppy is the principal source of opium, the dried latex produced by the [seed pods](/wiki/Ovary_(botany) "Ovary (botany)"). Opium contains a class of naturally occurring [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloids "Alkaloids") known as [opiates](/wiki/Opiates "Opiates"), that include [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine"), [oripavine](/wiki/Oripavine "Oripavine"), [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine").[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) The [specific epithet](/wiki/Botanical_name#Components_of_plant_names "Botanical name") *somniferum* means "sleep-bringing", referring to the [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative") properties of some of these opiates.[[79]](#cite_note-Bryant-79)

The opiate drugs are extracted from opium. The latex oozes from incisions made on the green seed pods and is collected once dry. Tincture of opium or [laudanum](/wiki/Laudanum "Laudanum"), consisting of opium dissolved in alcohol or a mixture of alcohol and water, is one of many unapproved drugs regulated by the U.S. [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") (FDA). Its marketing and distribution persists because its historical use preceded the Federal Food, Drug & Cosmetic Act of 1938.[[80]](#cite_note-IJPC-2006-80) Tincture of opium B.P., containing 1% w/v of anhydrous morphine, also remains in the [British Pharmacopoeia](/wiki/British_Pharmacopoeia "British Pharmacopoeia"),[[81]](#cite_note-81) [listed as a Class A](/wiki/List_of_controlled_drugs_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of controlled drugs in the United Kingdom") substance under the [Misuse of Drugs Act 1971](/wiki/Misuse_of_Drugs_Act_1971 "Misuse of Drugs Act 1971").

Morphine is the predominant alkaloid found in the cultivated varieties of opium poppy that are used for opium production.[[82]](#cite_note-INCB-82) Other varieties produce minimal opium or none at all, such as the latex-free Sujata type. Non-opium cultivars that are planted for drug production feature a high level of thebaine or oripavine. Those are refined into drugs like oxycodone. Raw opium contains about 8–14% morphine by dry weight, or more in high-yield [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar").[[28]](#cite_note-Kapoor-1995-28) It may be used directly or chemically modified to produce semi-synthetic opioids such as [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin").
